# Figure description: скв_387.jpg

**Source**: `36572_Smolianova_1984/drill_holes_data/скв_387.jpg`

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (геологический разрез скважины / каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log typical of Soviet-era mineral exploration practice. It is a long, vertically oriented document combining a lithological/stratigraphic column on the left with geophysical logging curves (электрокаротаж / well logging traces) in the central and right portions. The format is consistent with standard Soviet бланки (log forms) used during regional geological surveys.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log header (top centre) appears to read **"Уч-к Оксановский"** (Oksanovsky sector/site), suggesting this borehole is located within or near the **Oksanovsky prospect area** in northern Kazakhstan. A borehole number is visible in the header — partially legible as **скв [illegible number]**, likely a shallow-to-medium exploration borehole. The depth range covered appears to span from **0 m at the top** to approximately **several hundred metres** at the base (exact total depth [illegible] due to scan quality, but the log is very long, suggesting a depth of several hundred metres, possibly **400–600 m**). The bottom section is labelled **"Контрольные кривые"** (Control curves), a standard Soviet logging convention.

Map sheet context consistent with **M-42 or N-42** regional coverage.

---

## Key Content

The composite log contains the following observable columns and traces, from left to right:

**Left side — Lithological/Stratigraphic columns:**
- A narrow column with **stratigraphic age/formation symbols** (Cyrillic and standard geological notation), though largely illegible at this resolution.
- A **lithological pattern column** showing varying rock textures rendered in standard Soviet hatching/dot patterns — visible patterns suggest alternating sequences of:
  - **Dotted patterns** (sandstones or coarse clastic rocks)
  - **Horizontal line patterns** (clays, siltstones, or fine-grained sediments)
  - **Brick/block patterns** (possible carbonates or dense rocks)
- A **depth scale column** running vertically down the left side with depth markers, appearing to be graduated in **10-metre intervals**.
- A **sample/core description column** with handwritten or typed text annotations — largely [illegible] at this resolution.

**Central section — Geophysical logging curves:**
Two or three continuous waveform traces are plotted vertically through the central portion of the log. These appear to represent:
- **КС (Кажущееся сопротивление / Apparent resistivity)** — the broader, more variable curve showing significant deflections, characteristic of resistivity logging used to identify lithological boundaries and fluid-bearing zones.
- **ПС (Потенциал самополяризации / Spontaneous Potential / SP)** — a narrower, smoother curve running adjacent to the resistivity trace, used to identify permeable beds and lithological contrasts.
- Possibly a **gamma-ray (ГК)** or **neutron log** trace as a third curve on the right side of the central panel — this is consistent with standard Soviet triple-combo logging practice.

**Curve behaviour observations:**
- In the **upper portion** of the log, the curves show relatively **low-amplitude, moderate-frequency fluctuations**, suggesting relatively homogeneous or fine-grained sequences.
- In the **middle section**, there are several **prominent high-amplitude deflections** to the right (high resistivity anomalies), possibly indicating **dense, dry, or mineralised intervals** — carbonates, consolidated sandstones, or possibly ore-bearing zones.
- The **lower-middle section** shows a zone of **reduced amplitude and tighter oscillations**, potentially representing a clay-dominated or saturated interval.
- Near the **bottom** of the main log, before the control curves section, there are again **sharp high-amplitude spikes**, possibly indicating hard basement rocks or another significant lithological unit.

**Bottom section — "Контрольные кривые" (Control Curves):**
A separate panel at the very bottom shows repeat/control logging runs over a short interval, used to verify the quality and repeatability of the logging measurements. This is a standard Soviet QC procedure.

**Right side — Data table columns:**
Several narrow tabular columns on the right side appear to record:
- Interpreted lithological units or formation names
- Possibly **water-bearing horizon data** or **core recovery percentages**
- Interval-by-interval descriptions — largely [illegible] at this resolution

## Research Value

This composite borehole log provides **direct subsurface lithological and petrophysical data** for the Oksanovsky sector, allowing a geologist to identify stratigraphic boundaries, lithological units, and potentially mineralised or fluid-bearing intervals through resistivity and SP curve analysis.
